g.desc: This image shows what a completed Piper Computer Kit looks like.
g.title: Completed Assembly
Step 1 Disassembly: Remove Computer Kit Front Latch
g.desc: This is step one, and it shows how to remove the latch from the front of your Computer Kit. First, orient the kit so the front panel, which is the side that would face you, is tilted upwards. Feel along the top edge of this front panel to locate the latch assembly. It consists of two identical 5mm thick plywood pieces, stacked one on top of the other, secured by two small brass screws and two brass cylindrical standoffs underneath. The plywood pieces have a distinct anchor or 'T' shape with a central hole and rounded edges, which you can identify by touch. To remove the latch, use a screwdriver to unscrew and lift out the two small #4-40 brass screws from the top plywood piece. Once the screws are out, carefully lift off the top plywood piece, then the second identical plywood piece. Finally, remove the two brass standoffs that were beneath the plywood pieces. These will feel metallic. Place all removed components on your Components sheet for organization.

g.title: Step 2.1 Disassembly: Unscrew and Remove Top Plate
g.desc: This instruction shows you how to take off the top part of your kit. First, find the large, flat, rectangular top plate of your kit. It's made of 5mm plywood and might have some decorative etching in the center. Feel its outer edges; they have a wavy, interlocking shape where it connects to the main box below. Next, feel around the top plate for four small screw heads, one near each corner. These are your Type A brass screws – they feel small and metal, with a textured head for a screwdriver. Using a screwdriver, turn each screw counter-clockwise to loosen it. Continue turning until all four screws are completely unfastened and can be lifted out. As you remove the screws, also retrieve the four small, square brass nuts (part of the #4-40 hardware) that were holding them in place; they might fall free from the box's rim. Once all four screws and their matching nuts are removed, carefully lift the entire top plate straight up and away from the rest of the kit. This will expose the inside of your kit, which contains wires, electronics, and plastic components.

This step guides you to disconnect all cables from the Display Back module and the battery. First, locate the rectangular Display Back, which is a circuit board with several ports. Along one of its edges, feel for three cables that are plugged in. The first cable has a small, rectangular microUSB plug; gently pull this plug straight out to disconnect it. The second cable has a larger, rectangular HDMI plug; gently pull this plug straight out. The third cable has a slender, round metal Audio Jack plug; gently pull this plug straight out. Next, find the rectangular battery component. Locate the last cable that connects from the Display Back module to the battery. Gently pull the connector from the battery to disconnect it. Then, gently pull the other end of this same cable out of its port on the Display Back module. All cables should now be completely disconnected from both components.
g.title: Step 2.1 Disassembly: Disconnect Display and Battery Wiring
Step 2.2 Disassembly: Disconnect Raspberry Pi Cables
g.desc: This step shows how to disconnect three cables from your Raspberry Pi. First, locate the Raspberry Pi, which is a small, flat circuit board. On one of its shorter edges, find a small, circular port (the audio jack). Feel the cable with its thin, cylindrical metal plug connected to it. Gently pull this plug straight out to disconnect it. Next to the audio port, you will find a wider, flat rectangular port (the HDMI port). Feel the cable with its corresponding flat, rectangular plug and gently pull it straight out. Finally, on the same edge, locate a small, trapezoidal port (the MicroUSB or USB-C power port). Feel the cable with its small, angled plug and gently pull it straight out. The blueprint uses upward arrows from each port to its corresponding plug to illustrate these disconnections.

g.title: Disassemble: Remove Raspberry Pi Module
g.desc: This step shows how to remove the Raspberry Pi circuit board from the kit's bottom casing. First, locate the Raspberry Pi: it's a flat, rectangular electronic board, about the size of a credit card, covered with many tiny components and ports along its edges. You'll feel it securely fastened inside the open, 5mm thick plywood casing. It is held in place by four #4-40 brass screws, which you can find near each corner of the board. Use a screwdriver to turn each metallic screw counter-clockwise to loosen and remove it. Once all four screws are out, gently lift the Raspberry Pi module straight up. As you lift, notice it detaching from small, round standoffs that elevate it from the casing. To orient yourself, the Raspberry Pi is positioned above where you might feel a smooth, rectangular plastic piece and two smaller, white breadboards further towards the front of the casing.

g.title: Step 3 Disassembly: Separate Front Panel and Screen
g.desc: This instruction shows you how to separate the front panel and screen assembly from the main computer case. First, locate the four small, brass #4-40 screws, labeled 'A', on the bottom edge of the main case body, where it connects to the front panel. Use a screwdriver to turn these screws counter-clockwise until they are completely removed. The front panel, which has a large rectangular cutout for the screen and a smaller oval hole for a button, can then be lifted straight up and away from the main case. Inside the main case, you'll feel a smooth, rectangular plastic component (shown in blue) and two flat, rectangular breadboards with many small holes (shown in white). Remember, do not remove these breadboards or the blue plastic part; they stay attached to the main case.

g.title: Step 4.1 Disassembly: Remove Right Side Panel and Top
g.desc: You are now taking apart some sections of your computer kit. First, locate the large, flat 5mm thick plywood piece that forms the right side of your assembled kit. This panel has several distinct holes and cutouts. Along its top edge, feel for three small brass screws (Type A hardware). Use your screwdriver to carefully unscrew and remove these three #4-40 brass screws. Next, feel along the bottom edge of this same right side panel. On the inside, you will feel a blue plastic bracket. Locate a small #4-40 brass nut attached to a screw that passes through this plastic bracket and the plywood panel. Unscrew and remove this nut. With the three screws and the one nut removed, gently pull the entire right side plywood panel outwards and away from the rest of the kit. Place this panel aside with its removed hardware. Now, feel for the flat 5mm thick plywood piece that forms the very top cover of your kit. Gently lift this top panel straight upwards and off the assembly. Set it aside. Finally, reach inside the remaining structure. On the left inner wall, feel for a long, flat, rectangular blue plastic piece (labeled 2-6). Slide this piece out of its slot and set it aside. On the right inner wall, feel for a shorter, flat, rectangular blue plastic piece (labeled 2-7), located where the right side panel was attached. Slide this piece out of its slot and set it aside.

g.title: Step 4.2 Disassembly: Remove Toolbox from Computer Base
g.desc: This step details how to remove the internal toolbox module from the computer's main base. Begin by feeling for the brass #4-40 screw (labeled 'A') on the underside of the large, flat plywood base. Unscrew and remove it completely. This screw secures the upright plywood wall of the toolbox (labeled 2-3) to the base. The upright wall (2-3) has a rounded top edge and slots into the base; gently lift it straight up to disengage its tabs and then slide it away from the assembly. As you lift 2-3, a small horizontal brass screw on its left side and two small square brass nuts or standoffs underneath it will also be released from their connections. Next, locate the long, thin plywood piece (labeled 2-4) with small notches along its top edge, which connects to the upright wall. Gently lift and pull it away. Finally, slide out the smooth, rectangular plastic piece (labeled 2-5), which is solid and feels distinct from the plywood, from its position between the other toolbox components. All these parts are removed from the main base.

g.title: Step 4.2 Disassembly: Remove Computer Back Panel and Hardware
g.desc: This step instructs you to disassemble the back of your computer kit. First, locate the largest, flat plywood piece, which is the main body of the computer (labeled 2-1). Feel along its bottom edge for a small, threaded metal screw (labeled A). This is a #4-40 brass screw. Use a screwdriver to turn it counter-clockwise until it's completely removed. Next, find the long, rectangular plywood piece (labeled 2-2) that forms the back panel. Feel along its surface for a small, round, smooth plastic button or peg. Gently push or pull this plastic piece away from the panel to remove it. With both fasteners removed, carefully lift the back panel (2-2) straight up, separating its interlocking tabs from the slots on the main body (2-1).

g.title: Step 5.1 Disassembly: Screen/Lid Side Panel Disassembly
g.desc: This step guides you in disassembling the screen/lid assembly by removing its plywood side panels. You should have a central flat screen unit with attached electronics. Around it, feel for two long, flat 5mm thick plywood side panels with laser-cut edges and various notches. The diagram shows arrows indicating you need to unscrew and remove multiple #4-40 brass screws and corresponding hexagonal nuts. These screws have a small threaded shaft and a flat or Phillips head. Carefully unscrew all hardware along the edges, pulling the plywood side panels away from the central screen unit. The four smooth, light blue plastic standoffs holding the central electronics will remain attached.

g.title: Step 5.2 Disassembly: Remove Screen Module Fasteners
g.desc:
This step shows how to detach the screen module and its attached speaker from the main plywood panel by removing eight fasteners. First, identify the large, flat, 5mm thick plywood panel. In its central opening, you will feel the rectangular screen module, which has a distinct, smoother surface and a slightly raised edge. To the right of the screen module, locate the smaller rectangular plastic speaker module, which has a noticeable circular grate in its center.
The screen and speaker modules are held to the plywood panel by eight sets of hardware. Each set includes a small #4-40 brass screw, which feels like a tiny threaded rod with a flat head, and a blue, cylindrical plastic barrel nut, which feels smooth and has a threaded hole inside. The diagram shows these fasteners being removed.
Carefully locate and unscrew the eight brass screws. There are four screws along the left edge of the screen module (two near the top, two near the bottom), two along the top-left edge of the screen module, and two more along the top-right edge near the speaker module. As you unscrew them, the blue plastic barrel nuts will become loose on the back of the plywood panel. Once all eight screws and nuts are removed, gently lift and separate the screen module, with the speaker still attached to it, away from the large plywood panel.

g.title: Step 5.2 Disassembly: Disconnect Speaker Cable from Screen
g.desc: This step shows how to disconnect the speaker's audio cable from the screen module. First, locate the speaker, which is a flat rectangular piece of plywood. Feel for the circular speaker cone in the middle and the thick, flexible black wire coming out from it. Follow this wire to its end, where you'll find a plastic connector bent at a right angle, like the letter 'L'. At the end of this 'L' shape, feel for the shiny metal tip – this is the audio jack. Now, find the screen module, which is another rectangular piece, likely made of plywood or plastic. On one of its edges, you will feel a small round hole where the speaker's metal audio jack is currently plugged in. Gently grasp the 'L'-shaped plastic part of the audio jack and pull it straight out of the screen module's hole to disconnect it. You should feel it slide out with a small tug.
